paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 20: The Unsent Letter (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 4 boy helping his mother clear out his late grandmother's two-room HDB flat in Bukit Merah, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
It was a Saturday in June, six months after Ah Ma had passed away, and my mother had finally agreed to clear out the two-room flat in Bukit Merah. I had been told to come along because I was sixteen and tall enough to reach the top of the wardrobe, and, I suspected, because my mother did not want to do it alone. The flat smelled of medicated oil, old newspaper, and a faint sweetness my mother told me was tau sar piah, which Ah Ma had eaten every morning of my childhood.

Paragraph 2
We started in the kitchen. Pots into one box, kuali into another, chipped bowls stacked for the karang guni man. My mother worked in silence. She had not cried in the six months since the funeral, but every now and then she would pick up a thing — a wooden ladle, a tin of Milo — and hold it for slightly too long before placing it down.

Paragraph 3
By mid-morning we had reached the bedroom. The wardrobe was tall and dark, made of teak, with a smell inside it that was older than I was. I stood on the stool and pulled things from the top shelf. Folded sarong kebayas, samfu blouses wrapped in tissue paper, a Hokkien Bible with the spine half torn off. And right at the very back, pushed against the wood as though it had been deliberately hidden, a biscuit tin with a faded picture of two children on the lid.

Paragraph 4
I brought it down. The tin was light. I prised the lid off — inside there was no biscuit at all, only an envelope. Cream-coloured, slightly yellowed at the edges, and on the front, in Ah Ma's careful slanted handwriting, an address in Penang to a person called Aunt Hooi. The envelope was sealed. Where the stamp should have been, there was nothing.

Paragraph 5
I turned the envelope over. On the back, very small, Ah Ma had written a date: 14 February 1989. I felt the small, clean shock of arithmetic. The letter had been waiting in the tin for more than thirty years. I had not even existed when she had sealed it.

Paragraph 6
I carried it to the kitchen. 'Ma,' I said. I held it out. She wiped her hands on her trousers before she took it. She did not say anything for a long time. She looked at the address, then at the date, then at the address again. Her thumb passed once across Ah Ma's handwriting, the way you might brush dust off something fragile. She did not open it. She walked to the living room, took her handbag down from the hook, and slid the envelope carefully into the inside pocket where she kept her IC.

Paragraph 7
'We're going to Penang next Sunday,' she said. That was all. We carried on packing. By the time the karang guni man came in the afternoon, the kitchen was bare and the wardrobe empty, and the biscuit tin sat alone on the counter with its lid replaced, as though waiting to be remembered.

Paragraph 8
The following Sunday we drove across the Causeway before sunrise. My mother did not put on the radio. I did not ask whether she planned to open the envelope on the way. I knew she did not. The letter rode all the way to Penang in the inside pocket of her handbag, sealed, undisturbed, thirty-seven years late, addressed in a careful slanted hand to a younger sister who, I hoped, was still alive to receive it.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. tau sar piah: a traditional bean-paste biscuit; karang guni man: an informal rag-and-bone collector in Singapore; kuali: a large round cooking pan; samfu and sarong kebaya: traditional women's clothing; IC: Singaporean identity card.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, why does the narrator say he 'suspected' a second reason that his mother had asked him to come along? [1]
questions.answer: he sensed that his mother needed his emotional support as well as his physical help — she did not want to face the task of clearing out her own mother's flat by herself

questions.stem: Q2(a). What is suggested about Ah Ma by the phrase 'a faint sweetness... which Ah Ma had eaten every morning of my childhood' (Paragraph 1)? [1]
questions.answer: Ah Ma had very steady, settled habits — eating tau sar piah every single morning — and her presence has shaped the smell and feel of the flat over many years

questions.stem: Q2(b). What is effective about describing the mother as picking up objects and holding them 'for slightly too long' (Paragraph 2)? [1]
questions.answer: the small extra moment with each object shows that the mother is grieving silently — she is not crying openly, but each ordinary thing is heavy with memory of Ah Ma

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 3, what is the effect of saying the wardrobe had 'a smell inside it that was older than I was'? [1]
questions.answer: it stresses how much of Ah Ma's history is contained in the wardrobe — the smell has been accumulating for longer than the narrator's whole life, suggesting layers of time and memory inside it

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 3, why does the narrator describe the biscuit tin as being 'pushed against the wood as though it had been deliberately hidden'? [1]
questions.answer: the position of the tin suggests that Ah Ma had not just forgotten the letter — she had placed it out of sight on purpose, which makes the find feel more private and significant

questions.stem: Q5. What does the phrase 'the small, clean shock of arithmetic' (Paragraph 5) mean? [1]
questions.answer: as soon as the narrator does the maths between 1989 and the present he realises with a sudden, sharp clarity exactly how long the letter has been waiting — the shock is not loud or messy but precise and sudden

questions.stem: Q6(a). What does the word 'fragile' (Paragraph 6) suggest about how the mother handles the envelope? [1]
questions.answer: she treats the envelope with extreme care, as though it could be damaged by even a small touch — this reflects how precious and emotionally delicate the letter is to her

questions.stem: Q6(b). Why does the mother put the envelope into the inside pocket of her handbag 'where she kept her IC' (Paragraph 6)? [1]
questions.answer: she places it in the most safe and personal place she has, the same pocket as her identity card — this signals that she has decided the letter is too important to lose and is now her responsibility

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the very short sentence 'That was all.' at the start of Paragraph 7? [1]
questions.answer: the brief sentence underlines that no further discussion is needed — the decision to drive to Penang has been made quietly and completely, and any more words would only diminish the moment

questions.stem: Q8. In Paragraph 7, what is suggested by saying the biscuit tin 'sat alone on the kitchen counter with its lid replaced, as though waiting to be remembered'? [1]
questions.answer: the tin, now empty of the letter, takes on a quiet dignity of its own — it had been the keeper of the letter for over thirty years and now seems to be patiently waiting for the family to acknowledge what it had held

questions.stem: Q9. Why does the narrator say 'I did not ask whether she planned to open the envelope on the way. I knew she did not' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: he understands without asking that opening the letter on the way to Penang would be wrong — the letter belongs to Aunt Hooi, the person it was addressed to, and to read it first would be a kind of disrespect to Ah Ma's original intention

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of ending the passage with the words 'thirty-seven years late, addressed in a careful slanted hand to a younger sister who, I hoped very much, was still alive to receive it' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it closes the passage on a note of tenderness mixed with anxiety — the long delay underlines how much time has been lost, and the narrator's quiet hope that Aunt Hooi is still alive gives the journey a sense of urgency and sacred purpose

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling changes as the morning at Ah Ma's flat unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: curious / bored / solemn / frightened / resolved / impatient / reverent / suspicious. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Unsent Letter' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the morning — from curious as he prises open the biscuit tin and discovers the sealed envelope, to solemn as the date on the back tells him the letter has been waiting for over thirty years, to resolved alongside his mother as she places the envelope safely in her handbag and announces the trip, ending in a reverent silence in the car across the Causeway to Penang. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 3–4 as he pulls the biscuit tin from the very back of the wardrobe and prises off its lid to find the cream-coloured envelope inside? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5 as he turns the envelope over, reads the date '14 February 1989' on the back and realises the letter has been waiting in the tin for more than thirty years? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 6–7 as his mother slides the envelope into the IC pocket of her handbag and quietly announces, 'We're going to Penang next Sunday'? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as they drive across the Causeway before sunrise, the radio off and the sealed envelope riding undisturbed all the way to Penang? [1]
